wiki_events.headline: The European Union's Veterinarian Committee bans the export of British beef and its by-products as a result of mad cow disease (Bovine spongiform encephalopathy).

pages.title: European Union
pages.abstract: The European Union (EU) is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe. The supranational union has a total area of 4,233,255 km2 (1,634,469 sq mi) and an estimated population of more than 450 million as of 2025. The EU is often described as a sui generis political entity combining characteristics of both a federation and a confederation.

pages.title: Bovine spongiform encephalopathy
pages.abstract: Bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE), commonly known as mad cow disease, is an incurable and always fatal neurodegenerative disease of cattle. Symptoms include abnormal behavior, trouble walking, and weight loss. Later in the course of the disease, the cow becomes unable to function normally. There is conflicting information about the time between infection and onset of symptoms. In 2002, the World Health Organization suggested it to be approximately four to five years. Time from onset of symptoms to death is generally weeks to months. Spread to humans is believed to result in variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD) or Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD). As of 2024, a total of 233 cases of vCJD had been reported globally.

nytimes_articles.headline: Problems Schools Can't Solve
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One of my students wrote a short story in which he recounted how he watched as his father was shot and killed inside their apartment building. Another student was neglected so badly that at age 7 she lost all her hair from malnutrition; at 14, she still had to wear diapers to school. Yet another student was walking to the grocery store with her best friend when gang members started firing at one another; her best friend was killed. And just the  other day, a 9-year-old boy in one of my classes wrote: 

     "I am afraid I might die before I'm 11 . . . I hate myself because I'm dumb.

nytimes_articles.headline: Waiting for a Black Heathcliff
nytimes_articles.abstract:
African-Americans make up 12 percent of the population but 25 percent of the  movie-going audience, according to the March 18 issue of People magazine. What have our billions of dollars bought us? "A lot more than they used to," say some. "Not enough," says Jesse Jackson, who organized a nationwide protest last  night during the Academy Awards broadcast, calling attention to the "marginal role" of blacks in the film industry. 

     When I was working on and off Broadway in the 1960's and 70's, the black leading man of the day was Sidney Poitier, period. Today, we have Denzel Washington, Danny Glover, Forest Whitaker, Samuel L. Jackson, Wesley Snipes, Laurence Fishburne, Louis Gossett Jr., Morgan Freeman and James Earl Jones. Progress? You bet.

Waiting for a Black Heathcliff
nytimes_articles.abstract:
African-Americans make up 12 percent of the population but 25 percent of the  movie-going audience, according to the March 18 issue of People magazine. What have our billions of dollars bought us? "A lot more than they used to," say some. "Not enough," says Jesse Jackson, who organized a nationwide protest last  night during the Academy Awards broadcast, calling attention to the "marginal role" of blacks in the film industry. 

     When I was working on and off Broadway in the 1960's and 70's, the black leading man of the day was Sidney Poitier, period. Today, we have Denzel Washington, Danny Glover, Forest Whitaker, Samuel L. Jackson, Wesley Snipes, Laurence Fishburne, Louis Gossett Jr., Morgan Freeman and James Earl Jones. Progress? You bet.

nytimes_articles.headline: Problems Schools Can't Solve
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One of my students wrote a short story in which he recounted how he watched as his father was shot and killed inside their apartment building. Another student was neglected so badly that at age 7 she lost all her hair from malnutrition; at 14, she still had to wear diapers to school. Yet another student was walking to the grocery store with her best friend when gang members started firing at one another; her best friend was killed. And just the  other day, a 9-year-old boy in one of my classes wrote: 

     "I am afraid I might die before I'm 11 . . . I hate myself because I'm dumb.

Observer;Pluto, Polo, Oh Woe!
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A British scholar says Marco Polo never got to China. He may have made it to  Constantinople, she says, but he probably invented the tale about the rest of the trip. 

     This is another example of how everything you learned as a kid eventually turns out to be wrong. It was only a few days ago, for instance, that we learned Pluto may not be a planet. What next?
